Question: What support does the ASU Library provide for open access publishing?
Answer Text: ASU Library can provide waivers or discounts on APCs with certain publishers as a result of our partnerships and subscriptions. Additionally, we manage the ASU Open Access (OA) Publishing Fund, which supports ASU authors who wish to make their scholarly articles freely available immediately upon publication. All current ASU faculty, staff and students are eligible for funds, with a maximum award of $2000 per request. Applications to the fund will open in January 2026. For a complete current list of partnerships and the criteria and application instructions for the OA Publishing Fund, visit our Researcher Support open access publishing and support page. If making your work open access is important to you, you can publish in a traditional journal as long as you retain the rights to self-archive your work in a repository , such as our KEEP institutional repository or a disciplinary repository of choice.

Question: I would like to learn how to manage my research data throughout my project
Answer Text: Our Introduction to Research Data Management tutorial is the best place to start. It is a self-paced walkthrough of the essential concepts in research data management, including definitions, effective management, sharing strategies, and other tips to help keep you organized and track your process for reproducibility and recognition for your efforts. We also have a research data management guide , including support options, suggested readings, data management plan writing, and links to data management tutorials. The ASU Research Data Management Office also has additional information, mainly if you are working on a grant-funded project. After checking out these resources, let us know if you have any other questions and if you would like us to add you to an email list for potential workshops and events around research data management and sharing that we will hold in the future.

Question: I'm starting a group research project and have been asked to determine the best way to organize the research that will be done to provide continuity throughout the life of the project. Can you assist by recommending some information management tools?
Answer Text: It's great that your team is identifying their research management tools early in the project! There are many possible scenarios for working together as a team, so I'm going to share what I'd personally recommend investigating, but there are of course other options out there, depending on the preferences of your team members. First, I recommend Zotero as a great, collaborative citation manager with options to use as a team. Since it's open source, it's freely available to everyone across institutions, has an active user community and support , and plenty of tools - a web option, browser extensions, and a desktop client - to easily save and share your research review and format bibliographies and citations. There is a paid account option to add storage space, but if that's not in your team's budget, you can pair it with a shared Dropbox folder to store the pdfs or other files to keep the full text handy. Secondly, you might consider using OSF (Open Science Framework) as a project and information management tool for your team. You can sync a variety of storage and other tools in one place (OSF works with Zotero, Dropbox, Google Drive, and more). ASU is an institutional affiliate of OSF, so you can create accounts using your ASURITE log in information to make it easy to get started . OSF also has an active user community with a lot of guidance targeting research groups (for example, this getting started page ) The Citation Management Library Guide includes some other tools for managing your information - both Mendeley and Endnote are proprietary (rather than open source) tools, so will have more limitations on access and use depending on institutional affiliations of all your team members and subscription options. I personally prefer using open source tools when available and possible (and both OSF and Zotero are great tools!).

Question: What is a DOI?
Answer Text: A DOI is a Digital Object Identifier. It is a unique number that is assigned to an object that provides a persistent link to the object. https://www.doi.org/ This often applies to scholarly journal articles, but can apply to other items. A DOI eliminates issues with changes made to an object’s URL (web address). All DOIs begin with a "10." In citation styles, if available, a DOI is usually shown at the end of the citation. DOIs are visible in some databases, but "hidden" in others. Not every item nor journal article has a DOI. If you have a DOI, you can try our library Citation Linker to see if we have the article. Citation Linker Here is a link to our Find Articles page.

Question: Why are so many library items located off site in the High Density Collection facility?
Answer Text: To make room for student study spaces and other library services, we have relocated many books to our High Density Collection (HDC) facility, where we already store millions of volumes. HDC includes a patron request fulfillment center, and staff are able to pull books and deliver them to ASU campuses, often within a few hours, and usually within a day or two. Please feel free to use the "Request ASU Copy" link in the library item record to request most of these items. There is no limit on these requests for our ASU affiliates.

Question: Can someone else pick up my interlibrary loan items for me, on my behalf?
Answer Text: Yes, ASU students, faculty and staff may authorize another individual, as a “proxy”, to use their Library account to transact library business in their name. Community card borrowers are not eligible for this service. How to apply To apply for a proxy card, visit our Proxy Accounts page and print the application form. Conditions and agreements It is important to remember the following conditions when requesting this service: The named individual has your permission to use your library account to transact library business in your name. You are responsible for all library materials checked out using your ASU Library account.  This includes proper return to one of the ASU Library and any fines incurred. Proxies must present their own photo ID card and indicate that they are borrowing materials under Proxy at time of checkout. The status of all proxies is reviewed annually in May. Proxy status for all Teaching Assistants expires in May. To renew, submit a new form. You may give someone who is not a designated proxy permission to check out library materials with your ASU ID card. To do so, please have them present their own ID, your ASU ID and a signed, dated, written permission from you. Questions? Please contact the Circulation Department at circ@asu.edu or 480-965-3605 with any questions. More information: https://lib.asu.edu/access/proxy

Question: What is a microform?
Answer Text: Microforms are scaled-down reproductions of documents, journal issues, monographs, or schemata made for the purposes of transmission, storage, reading, printing, and scanning. Reading, printing, and scanning microforms requires a special reader (or software). Readers and software are available at select ASU Library locations . ASU Library owns microforms in the following formats: microfilm ( film reels) microfiche (film sheets) micro-opaque (stock cards)

Question: The citation I have for an article might not be correct. What do I do now?
Answer Text: If searching the internet doesn't help you discover the correct citation... Here are a few things to try within our library systems: Make sure you are signed in to the library (top right corner of the library home page ). Near the center of the home page, choose one of the following options from the drop down list to the right of the large search bar: One Search Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Google Scholar Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Journal Title Then type or paste the name of the journal into the search bar. Click SEARCH. Select the result, if any, for the online version of the journal. If there is a link to the publisher's website , click down the list in the following order until you get to the Table of Contents at the Issue level: Year Volume Issue Look through the Table of Contents listing in page number order. Note: The article you need may actually fall within the pages of a different article. This happens frequently with book reviews!! Open the full text and look for the starting page of your article, just in case it is actually within another article! Another way to search for the correct citation... On the library home page, scroll down and click the View Resources icon. In the drop down list, choose: Web of Science Try the Cited Reference Search Try last name of the Author and the name of the Journal Note: Web of Science indexes the reference lists from articles in various journals and books, but it does NOT check to see if the citations are correct! So... If you find variations of the citation, the correct one usually has the highest citation count! The others typically have a mistake in one or more of the following: year, volume, issue and/or page number. If you need help, try our Ask a Librarian services!

Question: Technical support...details that help us troubleshoot your technical issues...
Answer Text: When submitting a ticket about a technical problem with ASU Library resources , please include as much detail as possible. The following information is especially helpful: Are you at home, at work, or on campus when the difficulty occurs? Are you logged into your ASUrite account? What Operating System are you using? (Windows 8, macOS 10.12, etc.) What Browser and Version Number are you using? (Internet Explorer 11, Firefox 52.2.1esr, etc.) Description of the steps you followed and at which screen the problem or error message appeared What is the URL (http://...) at the point of the error? Exact wording of any error messages Any screenshots that you may think would be helpful A telephone number where you may be reached during business hours In the meantime, here are a few things to try: Switch to a different browser (one may work better than another for a particular database or application: Firefox, Chrome, Internet Explorer, etc.) If you are coming in through MyASU, try starting from the library home page https://lib.asu.edu and then make sure you are signed in at the top right corner of the library page.

Question: Where can I find a permanent link to an item I found in One Search?
Answer Text: To find the persistent URL or permalink for a record you found in One Search... In the short display The short display is usually part of your results list as a numbered item (such as the number 3 item in your results list). Along the top right of the short display for the record, look for and click the three dots icon (...). In the resulting list of action options, click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use . In the full record display From the short record in the results list, click the title of the item until you find the longer full record for the item.  If there were multiple formats listed in the short record, you may need to click on the title in the new numbered list of short records, and then click on the title of an item to get to the full record! Look for the "Send to" section of the record to find the permalink. Then click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use.

Question: What is ASU Library’s Home Delivery Program, and how does it work?
Answer Text: The Home Delivery Program allows ASU students, faculty, and staff to request most physical ASU Library materials for delivery to their home address via FedEx shipping. For those on Native Lands or Rural Routes, materials are shipped through the United States Postal Service. Home Delivery service is available to ASU affiliates who live outside the Phoenix metropolitan/Maricopa County area but within the continental United States, Alaska, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico . Requesting items: When requesting ASU Library materials through the ASU Library website, please select "Personal Delivery" from the dropdown menu. Enter your complete home address for each item requested. Shipping details: ASU Library materials will be shipped by FedEx 2-day express shipping excluding weekends and Federal Holidays. USPS shipping averages approximately 5 business days. Q: What is NOT included in ASU Library’s Home Delivery Program? ASU Library Home Delivery is separate from the following services: Interlibrary Loan (ILL) Home Delivery – For ILL requests, visit: ILL Home Delivery . ASU Bookstore Rental Program – For rental inquiries, visit: ASU Bookstore Rental FAQ .

Question: I see "No records found" in One Search after searching a very common topic. What's up?
Answer Text: If you searched for a keyword that should have yielded results (for instance, searching the keyword Arizona yields millions of records), here are a few things to try: Adjust your search: Check your spelling; replace abbreviations with the actual words they represent. Start with simple search term(s). Start broadly and then narrow your search. Search for partial titles with quotation marks around phrases if the full title doesn't work. Remove punctuation (commas, periods, hyphens, colons, semi-colons, etc.). Still not working? Switch to a different browser to see if that works. Is the Personalization feature turned on? Check the results page (above the section where results would usually show). Clicking the word "Personalized" will turn it off and reset it back to "Personalize". Clear your browser cache (Click the link to learn how to clear your cache.) Then try your search again . If that doesn't work, use the Get Help! feature on our Library Systems Support page. Be sure to include the following information: Which browser you were using (Chrome, Firefox, Internet Explorer, Safari). What computer operating system you were using. And tell us if you were using the ASU Library site from on campus or off campus when you noticed the problem.

Question: How do I add people outside of ASU to manage my ASU Research Data Repository datasets?
Answer Text: With our current ASU Research Data Repository authentication process, only submitters with asu.edu emails can create an account. Anyone outside the university can view and access published collections (known as Dataverses), but cannot directly submit/deposit or manage the metadata. If you have members of your project outside of ASU, you can have them submit their datasets and documentation to a secure sharing space like Google Drive or Dropbox and then upload the files on their behalf. If more direct interaction is desired, ASU sponsors can request that collaborators get an “affiliate ID” (ASURITE) to access ASU systems. The ID expires after one year and has to be renewed by the ASU sponsor at that time. To sponsor and request an ASU ID for your collaborators, fill out the ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request - Add or Renew form . Additional workarounds for research teams may include using the Dataverse API , research notebooks like LabArchives , or the OSF as a collaboration platform, and connecting the OSF to Dataverse (which uses an API token). For information on adding co-owners, see our FAQ entry on adding users or co-owners to my datasets .

Question: What type of files can I upload to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer Text: The ASU Research Data Repository (powered by Harvard's Dataverse platform) is ideal for depositing and sharing research data and supports a variety of research data types and formats. You are welcome to upload research data, supplementary tables, documentation, publications associated with data, presentations, etc. The Repository supports the uploading of any file type, including shapefiles, images, Flexible Image Transport System files, and compressed files. However, to make use of the Data Explorer feature, you need to upload tabular data files (e.g., Stata, SPSS, R, Excel (xlsx), and CSV). Some files will generate an archival file format for viewing within the repository, but users will have the option to download either the original or archival version. See the Dataverse Platform User Guide for more information.

Question: How can I get help accessing or using Overleaf Professional LaTex editor?
Answer Text: For help with Overleaf Professional, please visit ASU Research Computing's workshop overview page , which includes office hours, Slack information, and a workshop schedule. You can also check out the Overleaf FAQ and Help page . Access Overleaf at www.overleaf.com/edu/asu#overview . About Overleaf at ASU The Knowledge Enterprise at Arizona State University with W.P. Carey School of Business, Fulton Schools of Engineering, and The College of Liberal Arts and Sciences provide Overleaf Professional for all faculty, students, and staff who want to use a collaborative, online LaTeX editor for their projects. Some benefits of the Overleaf product are that it is a collaborative tool, offers history and track changes features, has a common compiler for reproducibility, offers centralized cloud storage for files, and can be integrated with Dropbox and GitHub. Overleaf is commonly used for: collaborating on research papers, publications, and proposals production of professional-looking mathematical equations automated cross-referencing automatic formatting

Question: Does ASU offer resources for students with disabilities?
Answer Text: Accessibility Resources ASU Library offers a variety of assistive software and equipment for our users. See our ASU Library Accessibility Services webpage for information.

Question: What items can I request through interlibrary loan? What can’t I request?
Answer Text: You can request these types of items through interlibrary loan: Books Book chapters (up to 3 per book) Journal articles (up to 3 per journal issue) Government documents Theses and dissertations Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks These items are not available to request: Textbooks Items for personal/non-academic use Entire journal issues Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks There is no limit to the amount of requests you can submit.  However, we recommend that you only request materials you can use in a reasonable amount of time to avoid overdue fees.  Renewals are not usually available. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill

Question: What is included in Distinctive Collections?
Answer Text: Rare or special materials that include manuscripts, books, photographs, media, ephemera, oral histories and artifacts from the following repositories: Chicano/a Research Collection Theatre for Youth and Community Collection Design and the Arts Special Collections Greater Arizona Collection Latin Americana Rare Books and Manuscripts Related collections include materials from the following repositories: Community-Driven Archives Initiative Labriola National American Indian Data Center University Archives

Question: What if the scan I receive is incorrect, missing pages, or illegible?
Answer Text: Responding directly to your email notification or your service ticket will reopen the request. Please be descriptive in your response as to the specific issue, ensuring ASU Library staff can effectively resolve the issue. Also please note that ILL and digitization services cannot guarantee a pristine or printable copy. Microform sources are analog media formats and are lacking in features considered standard in digital media. Microform formats are fragile, easy to corrupt and mutilate resulting in sub-optimal reproduction. Photographic illustrations reproduce poorly in microform format, with loss of clarity and halftones. Storage and reproduction increase degradation of the film or card source over time.

Question: If I know the title of an article, how do I find it?
Answer Text: If you know the title of the article... From the library home page , try the One Search dropdown to either One Search or Google Scholar . Enter the title of the article in the search bar. Put quotation marks around the title to force it to read as a phrase. Click: Search Library Your article title usually appears in the first few results. You can also search for a free (and legal) Open Access copy of the full text through Open Access Button . Current ASU student, faculty or staff: For our full text options, make sure your are signed in to the library system with your ASURITE ID and password. If a full text option is not available, the library will try to get a digital copy of the article for you through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: When you are on our ASU campuses, many of our full text online materials may be accessed using our ASU Guest wifi or from an ASU Library guest computer. Or check with your local public library or an academic institution with which you are affiliated. They may have Interlibrary Loan services that serve your needs.

Question: Why do I need to login to the library system?
Answer Text: Here are a few benefits when you sign in to the ASU Library system before performing a search: If an item is unavailable, logged-in users will see a link to request the item. You can usually Request ASU Copy, Request Chapter Scan, or Request from External Library (Interlibrary Loan). An email will either alert you when the item is ready for pickup or it will contain the digitized article or chapter scan! Logged-in users will see the full results that may also show which years are available for the journal or periodical. Logging in shows your loan period based on your ASU affiliation such as undergraduate, graduate, faculty or staff. You will also be able to see results from our databases , if you are logged in. If you click on the Accounts section of the library home page, it will sign you in to the following accounts: My Interlibrary Loan (ILLIAD) Account My Library Account Where you can view a list of your checked out items, R enew items that are eligible for renewal, And perform other account maintenance tasks. To sign in to the library system: Go to the ASU Library home page . In the upper right corner of the home page, click: Sign In

Question: I am finishing my ASU dissertation / thesis. Why isn't my remote access working?
Answer Text: ASU thesis or dissertation writers must be enrolled for at least 1-credit hour at ASU in order to retain online access and checkout privileges: The 1-credit dissertation / thesis course satisfies this requirement. Note: To retain library privileges over the summer, students must pre-register for Fall classes before the end of Spring semester.

Question: Do I need to be an ASU student to use the library? (Visitor Information)
Answer Text: Public visitors are welcome at any of our eight locations on the four ASU campuses. Hours of operation vary depending on the library and the time of year ( library locations and find contact information ). While you are in the library, you may connect your devices to the ASU Guest wireless network . The ASU Library is open to the public and home to our research collections, open stack collections at every library, and a variety of units and centers designed to engage learning and collaboration for all. Community Borrowing Cards are available for purchase for non-ASU affiliated library users, which provides borrowing privileges for most items from the open stacks collections.  In addition, the ASU Library houses a rich collection of rare, unique and distinctive materials . Get started by speaking with our experts at Ask an Archivist. Please note, building access to our Downtown Phoenix campus Library is limited to current ASU students, staff and faculty with active Sun Cards. Members of the public and Community Borrowers should call 602-496-0300 to arrange a visit. More information can be found on the Services for Visitors web page.

Question: How can I create my own account to Times Higher Education (THE)?
Answer Text: Current ASU faculty, staff and students can sign up for your own Times Higher Education (THE) account! Registering as a first time user: For anywhere, anytime access to the Times Higher Education, you first need to register using ASU email address. Please follow these instructions so that your personal THE account is associated with the University’s institutional subscription. From the Times Higher Education website click on the user account icon, i.e. the red person icon towards the top right. Register using your Arizona State University email address @asu.edu (Your username will appear in public facing parts of the site such as comments on articles.) Choose a user name and password. Click on Join us at the bottom and you will be logged in. You will receive a confirmation email. Once you have successfully registered your personal account, just click on Login the next time you use the service. Access digital editions: To access digital editions of THE, go to their homepage. Click the Professional tab, then click Digital Editions. Then simply select the issue you want to view. Download the app: The Times Higher Education app is available on iOS, Android and Kindle Fire. Visit your app store provider to download it to your device. Select the edition you would like to view, e.g. UK or Global Log in by clicking on the icon in the top right corner of their home page. Select Account, then click: Existing THE account Enter your username and password.

Question: How do I access government information and government documents?
Answer Text: ASU Library's Government Documents staff can assist in identifying and locating government information resources. Use the GovDoc-Info form to contact us. ASU students, faculty and staff can request government documents directly from the ASU online catalog . Public and non-ASU users can request government documents, found through the ASU online catalog , by filling out the Request Materials form , and may be picked up by scheduling an appointment using the GovDoc-Info form . Members of the public may use a library workstation to access government information online by scheduling an appointment using the GovDoc-Info form . Are there limitations on access? ALL users must follow acceptable ASU Library rules and procedures. Only ASU affiliates and community borrowing cardholders may borrow / check out our government documents.

Question: Can ASU Library help me with a legal matter?
Answer Text: ASU Library staff cannot provide legal assistance, such as finding expert witnesses;  interpreting cases, statutes, or regulations; identifying legal forms to use or help in filling out legal forms; and similar activities. We advise consulting an attorney in such cases, and consulting a law library for legal research assistance. ASU Library is separate from ASU Law Library, which provides its own Ask a Law Librarian service.

Question: I am working on a data management plan. Can you help me identify an appropriate data repository?
Answer Text: The best place to search for disciplinary repositories is: re3data.org Search tip: Searching for "education" in subjects provides a list of education related repositories . Researchers in the Julie Ann Wrigley Global Institute of Sustainability can also contact the Global Sustainability Support Services for information on GIOS data management. A non-disciplinary option is: Open Science Framework This includes both project management and data sharing. ASU now has an institutional account. To access: Go to osf.io Select sign in (at the top right of the page) Choose "Sign in through my institution" Arizona State is the first option in the drop down list. However, if you have any sensitive information this may not be the final sharing mechanism for you. If you are in the process of writing a plan: DMPTool This tool provides templates for actively building data management plans . Login with your ASU credentials. You can also review our library guide on research data management for additional information, tutorials, and instructions. Note: DMPTool has been updated recently with improved functionality. For additional information or help: Fill out the Library's research service request form to set up a consultation appointment!

Question: How do I add users or co-owners to my datasets in the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer Text: We are using ASU single sign-on as the only method for signing in, and users add their accounts. Library admins cannot add them before they sign in for the first time, and users have read-only access when they are initially added. ASU Library staff provide permissions. Please contact the library and notify us which user you need added, after they have signed into the repository explorer page for the first time. Accounts are created at the time of first sign-in, and after that, the library repository administration team can add them to the account with the appropriate permission levels and even assign them to groups. If you only want to list someone as an author, but not grant permissions to manage your datasets, add their name in the metadata author fields and there is no need for them to sign into the repository. In a nutshell: You invite contributors by giving them the repository link, and they sign in to create an account, and then you request that we grant them access. The library cannot create accounts for them before they sign in. See Roles and Permissions in the Dataverse Platform Users Guide

Question: My book has been recalled and I can't return it on time! What can I do?
Answer Text: All ASU Library items are subject to recall. The recall policy applies to all users and is strictly enforced. It is in effect throughout the year including breaks, sabbaticals and vacations. When a checked out item is requested or recalled, items are due two weeks from the date it was requested by another user. Please review our Recall Policy page and our Returning Library Materials page for details on returning items. If a recalled item is returned after the recall due date, a late fee is charged. If you cannot return a recalled item by the recall due date, return the book to the library as soon as you are able. You may appeal library fines through a written appeal process after the item has been returned . Consult the Billing Appeal Procedure page for more information on how to appeal a fine. For contact information, please review our Billing Contact page.

Question: What online streaming video platforms are available through ASU Library, and how can I access them?
Answer Text: ASU Library provides access to thousands of streaming videos through three major providers: Alexander Street Films on Demand Kanopy Streaming videos are available in many other library-licensed collections and open collections. To find streaming videos in these collections, use the A-Z Databases module and limit to Database Type = Streaming Video . Searching this way will allow you to view a list of all of our current databases which contain a significant amount of streaming video content. For faculty, instructors and course designers: All videos in collections licensed by ASU Library can be used in ASU course management shells (Canvas, Learning Studio, Blackboard, etc.), syllabi, documents and other webpages. The ASU Library Resource Organizer is the best method for ensuring consistent links and access. ASU faculty interested in using the library’s streaming course reserve system to digitize video that is otherwise not available online should consult the Streaming Video Reserve Guidelines page. For detailed information about how to use videos in teaching, view this tutorial . Technical assistance: If you are unable to access a video, please submit a request through library systems support . Please indicate the specific video you are unable to access, and include information on your operating platform/device (Mac, PC, tablet, etc.) and browser.

Question: Does ASU offer any plagiarism checkers?
Answer Text: ASU currently uses Turnitin (TII) as part of the Canvas Classroom Management Software. Instructors - Turnitin must be activated for each assignment. Check ASU’s How to Enable Turnitin for Online Assignments information page! Students - Check your assignment in Canvas to see if Turnitin has been activated.

Question: Where can I park on the ASU campus?

Question: How do I decide which database to use for my search?
Answer Text: Choosing a database: Databases by subject or type: Go to the A SU Library home page Click on the tab: Databases Under Select databases by subject or type , use the drop-down lists to filter by Subject or by Type. Our librarians have created lists of recommended databases for particular research subjects , courses, and assignments . Our Frequently Used Resources are linked on the ASU Library home page using the red View Resources button . Take a look at our complete list of our ASU Library databases .

Question: I just want to find something fun to read.
Answer Text: Want something fun to read?  Do you have a title or favorite author? Although you can use other methods, we find that the Advanced Search option works best for searching our catalog for a book title or author. Here's how it works... From the library home page https://lib.asu.edu/ Just below and to the right of the main search box, you will find the Advanced Search option: Click:  Advanced Search Change "All resources" to: ASU Library Catalog Change "Any field" to: author or title Change "contains" to: is (exact) or starts with Fill in the blank with the author's name or the title of the book Hit <Enter> on your keyboard to search! If you want to simply search for a broad category of books: Click:  Advanced Search Change "All resources" to: ASU Library Catalog Change "Any field" to: subject Change "contains" to: is (exact) Fill in the blank with the category / genre of book you want, such as: fiction or mystery or romance or science fiction autobiographical fiction or biographical fiction or historical fiction graphic novels or novels or short stories plays or poems or poetry Hit <Enter> on your keyboard to search! Note: If you are looking for FICTION, all of our fiction is arranged by language, then eras, countries, and then by author. The Library of Congress (LC) classification system is designed for academic research rather than for browsing for leisure reading; therefore, LC subject terms are designed to find books by themes and other topics assigned within a literature call number classification.

Question: How long does it take to access materials?
Answer Text: We ask that you make requests for materials at least five (5) business days in advance, which will allow us to identify, secure, and move items to the appropriate reading room. An archives specialist will respond to all requests within 24 hours (Monday thru Friday, between 8 a.m. and 5 p.m.) to begin the process of fulfilling your request for materials, and will work with you to schedule your appointment. Contact Ask an Archivist for more information.

Phone: 480-965-6144

Question: What if I don't know the volume, issue, or reel number?
Answer Text: Here are a couple of things that may help identify information to help fulfill your request: Review the source of your citation for additional information, such as: alternative journal titles and/or updated ISSN Do a search on the internet for article title that may provide a citation. Do a search on the internet for journal title (and alternative journal titles) Occasionally material from older publications may be archived and available for download. If those options don't help you discover useful information to fulfill your request here are a few things to try within our library systems: Navigate to the library home page . Near the center of the home page, choose one of the following options from the drop down list to the right of the large search bar: One Search Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Google Scholar Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Journal Title Then type or paste the name of the journal into the search bar. Click SEARCH. Select the result, if any, for the online version of the journal. If there is a link to the publisher's website , click down the list in the following order until you get to the Table of Contents at the Issue level: Year Volume Issue Look through the Table of Contents listing in page number order. Note: The article you need may actually fall within the pages of a different article. This happens frequently with book reviews!! Open the full text and look for the starting page of your article, just in case it is actually within another article! If you need additional help, try our Ask a Librarian services!

Question: How will I receive my notifications or scanned request?
Answer Text: If you've submitted your request via ILLiad : All notifications will be sent to the email address provided in your ILLiad account. Please read the notification carefully as it may provide instructions to access your requested material through alternative means, or it may ask for additional information to continue processing your request. If your request can be fulfilled, the notification will include a link to log into your ILLiad account to obtain your scanned request. If you've submitted your request via a service ticket through the ASU Library Ask a Librarian Service : Your fulfilled request and any notifications will come as replies to your ticket submission. If your request is able to be fulfilled it will be attached as a PDF file.

Question: I lost library materials that I checked out. What happens next?
Answer Text: If the item that you lost belongs to the library: The Billing Department will send you a bill with the replacement cost for the lost material. Cost varies by item - Consult with Library Billing Unit. If the item that you lost is an ILL request: For interlibrary loan items that are not returned by the due date, there is an overdue fee of $5.00 per day (starting on the due date) with a maximum overdue fee of $25.00 and a default $150.00 replacement fee. The replacement fee may be adjusted depending on the actual cost of the item billed by the library who owns the item. We have a high overdue fee as many libraries will block us from requesting any materials when their items are returned late. If you find the lost item: You can return items that you have found back to the ASU Library. A full cost of replacement will be refunded if library material is returned in good condition within 6 months of billing. Payments cannot be refunded after 6 months . For more information about lost materials, visit our Overdues, Fines, and Fees website . Library Billing Department: PO BOX 871006 Tempe, AZ 85287-1006 480-965-3417 lillie.johnson@asu.edu

Question: How do I search for articles in a specific magazine?
Answer Text: Finding an article - Journal Title Search & search within publication: First, go to Journal Title Search https://lib.asu.edu/find-journals In the top box, type the name of the journal and hit "submit." The results will tell you the dates for any full text coverage in a particular database link. Click on the link to one of the databases that includes the dates for full text for your search needs. Find the SEARCH THIS PUBLICATION option on the resulting page and use keywords to search within this journal. Current ASU students, faculty and staff: If an article is not available as full text, you may wish to request a digital copy of the article through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: ASU Library Interlibrary Loan card (ILL card): Here is a link to information about the ASU Library ILL card for guest users https://lib.asu.edu/policies/communityloan/eligibility Or check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated; they may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

Question: How do I search for an article if I have the name of the journal, the year, volume, and issue?
Answer Text: To find a specific article, use the Journal Title Search to find the journal , then filter within the journal by year, volume and issue: First, go to Journal Title Search https://lib.asu.edu/find-journals In the top box, type the name of the journal and hit "submit." The results will tell you the dates for any full text coverage in a particular database link. Click on the link that contains the date for the article you need. The results will either give you a list of individual years or a group of years. Click on the link that contains the year for your article. The results will usually give you a list of volumes and/or issues. Click on the volume and/or issue you need. When you have reached the issue that you need, the results should be listed in Table of Contents order. Scroll down the list until you find the title (or page number) for your article. Current ASU students, faculty and staff: If an article is not available as full text, you may wish to request a digital copy of the article through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: ASU Library Interlibrary Loan card (ILL card): Here is a link to information about the ASU Library ILL card for guest users https://lib.asu.edu/policies/communityloan/eligibility Or check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated. They may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

Question: Who can access full text online?
Answer Text: Who can access full text online? Current ASU students, faculty and staff: Remember, you must be signed in to access most ASU Library resources from off-campus! Sign in to ASU Library with your ASURITE username and password. The Sign In link is in the upper right corner of the library home page. If you encounter a Sign In reminder within a pale yellow text box on our library pages... Sometimes the library system will prompt you again to Sign In. For additional access or request options, click the "Sign In" link to refresh the sign in connection. Click here for more information on our remote access policy . If you are working for an ASU department, but do not have remote access to ASU Library resources, click here for more information about the process for your designated affiliation. The affiliate status designated by your department controls your ability to access remote library databases and services. Access is not controlled by the library. Arizona residents: Online resources for Arizona residents are available online through the Digital Arizona Library (DAZL) . Note: DAZL is made available by the State of Arizona's Arizona State Library, Archives and Public Records . It is the State of Arizona's library system, which is not affiliated with Arizona State University's ASU Library system.

Question: I have an ED number from the ERIC database. Is there a quick way to tell if it is available in full text?
Answer Text: ERIC (Education Resources Information Center) is an education database that is available through ASU Library's online subscription services ( https://libguides.asu.edu/ericproq ) ( https://libguides.asu.edu/ericeduc ), but a public version of ERIC is available at: http://eric.ed.gov Note:  ERIC indexes educational journal articles as well as educational documents.  ERIC documents are assigned an ED number that is followed by 6 digits. There is a quick way to search the public version (ERIC website) to determine whether it contains fulltext of the ERIC digest article / ERIC document. Fill in the blank with the 6 digit ED number and use this URL: http://files.eric.ed.gove/fulltext/ED______.pdf Note: Sometimes authors do not give ERIC permission to post copies of their works, but you can check our ASU Library holdings for a copy of the work. It will not be listed by ED number, but as the actual work such as by title and author.

Question: What types of resources can I find in ASU Library's One Search?

Journal articles, including: open access full-text online peer-reviewed and more Books online (ebooks) physical books Audio Visual materials, including: images videos audio recordings Journals Databases Library Guides Dissertations Reviews Articles Book chapters Music scores Newspaper articles Reference entries Conference proceedings Government documents Legal documents Maps Web Sites Patents Statistical data sets Research data sets Technical reports And more under: Other Text Resources

Question: Can I use the library system from off campus?
Answer Text: Visitors: Many online resources provided through the ASU Library’s web site are available to visitors while visiting the library in person by using the ASU Guest wireless network . Please use the ASU for You platform to explore publicly accessible, digital educational resources, curated by the ASU Library, that are available to everyone, everywhere, and can be integrated into your academic and professional work. Find more information on the Services for Visitors web page. Question: How can I get anywhere anytime access to the Wall Street Journal?
Answer Text: WSJ.com subscriptions are available to current Arizona State University faculty, staff and students. How to activate your digital subscription Go to the WSJ registration page: WSJ.com/ArizonaState Enter your first and last name Select an Account Type from the dropdown: Student, Professor, or Staff Enter your ASU email address and create a password. The email address and password will allow access on other devices outside of the university network. Click Create to complete registration and create your WSJ subscription. Once you have an account you can go directly to: https://www.wsj.com/ When does my digital subscription expire? If you’re a student: When you activate your student membership, you will be prompted to input their graduation date & year. This is your membership expiration date. If you happen to graduate later than expected and lose access, you can re-activate your membership. If you’re Faculty/Staff: All faculty & staff retain WSJ membership for one year after activating. After that, you will have to re-activate your membership. What if I already have a WSJ subscription? Current subscribers can call 1-800-JOURNAL (1-800-568-7625) to switch from their paid subscription to the membership through their university and they will be refunded the remaining balance of their subscription. What your digital subscription includes Access to wsj.com articles 1997 to present WSJ mobile apps for your device from Apple App Store or Google Play Store . A newsletter of curated content sent to your inbox every month (optional) What about older WSJ articles? Older articles (1984 - present) can be accessed via the Wall Street Journal (ProQuest) database.

Question: How do I fix the "400 Bad Request: Request Header or Cookie Too Large" error when accessing library resources?
Answer Text: This error often relates to issues with your browser’s cookies or settings. Try the following troubleshooting steps to resolve the problem: Quick Fix. Test in an Incognito/Private Window: Open an incognito or private browsing window and try accessing the resource again. If this works, the issue is likely with your browser’s cookies. Proceed to clear your cache and cookies. Clear Cache and Cookies : Ensure you clear them for all time. Close your browser after clearing, reopen it, log in to MyASU, and try again. Switch Browsers: If the issue persists, try accessing the resource using a different browser. Check Your VPN Connection: Sign out of your VPN and sign back in. Note: Using a VPN does not provide direct access to ASU Library resources. Access is only supported through the ASU Library portal by signing in to your ASU account on the library website or using One Search . Restart Your Device and Network: Disconnect your internet connection. Restart your computer. Reconnect and try again. Check for Blockers: Disable any firewalls, pop-up blockers, or browser extensions that could interfere with access. Try a Different Device or Network: Access the resource from another device or a different internet connection to determine if the issue is device- or network-specific Still having trouble? If these steps do not resolve the issue, please contact us for support. Be sure to include screenshots or a video that shows the full URL in the browser.

The full text often appears in those additional versions. Need more help? Contact us! Email, call, or chat with library staff through Ask a Librarian . Report an error . Include the steps you took to experience the error and include screenshots, if possible.

Question: What is the best license for my dataset in the ASU Research Data Repository? Should I use CC-by?
Answer Text: We recommend using CC0 and adding a suggested citation because in most cases data (collection of facts) might not be eligible for copyright protection and ultimately creates an unnecessary barrier to re-use. For further reading see This post from the University of California’s Office of Scholarly Communication provides an overview of the situation and recommends the CC0 license for many kinds of data. The Open Database License (ODC-By) also has an attribution variant. See also our quick guide on licensing and data .

Question: I have been using the original Harvard Dataverse. Can my datasets or dataverse be moved to ASU?
Answer Text: Yes. The ASU Library can work with you to copy your Harvard Dataverse to ASU's Research Data Repository . To maintain your original statistics, we will leave the original datasets in Harvard and create new copies at ASU. A metadata record will point back to the Harvard repository. We welcome ASU researchers who have been using the Harvard interface to work with us, unless there are mitigating circumstances like journal, funder, or collaboration requirements that require Harvard's platform.  Working with our team results in greater documentation and replicability of your work. You can explore datasets in the repository to see examples of datasets curated by our staff. To submit a migration request or have questions about the process, please get in touch with ASU Library's Researcher Support and request a consultation.

Answer Text: Use filters (located on the right side of the results) to see fewer items and items that better match your needs. Question: Is there a default or standard metadata schema I can use to describe and organize my research data?
Answer Text: There is not a default metadata standard for research data. Good planning and consistency with your file names, keywords, names, and dates will result in well-described and well-organized data. This alone may create adequate metadata for your project. There are general and discipline-specific metadata schemas available. Your schema will be informed by the research question you are investigating, your disciplinary focus, or even where you will publish. The DataCite Metadata Schema is a general schema that may be helpful for you. The Data Documentation Initiative (DDI) is an international standard for describing data produced by surveys and other observational methods in social, behavioral, economic, and health sciences. The Digital Curation Centre (DCC) provides a list of disciplinary metadata schema that may be appropriate for your project. To learn more about research data management: Watch the Research Data Management Basics tutorial. Read our library guide on research data management to learn more about metadata best practices and other strategies. For help with your project: Request a consultation by filling out the online request form.

The Arizona Republic archive is not affiliated with ASU or ASU Library. Current ASU students, faculty and staff may request reels or specific articles through ASU Library's Interlibrary Loan/Document Delivery service. Question: How do I create Digital Object Identifier (DOI) for my materials?
Answer Text: Here are a few ways you can obtain a Digital Object Identifier (DOIs) for your materials: Repository Options You can upload your work to a repository that provides DOIs for uploaded works. These can include disciplinary repositories , such as CORE-Humanities Commons or bioRxiv , as well as interdisciplinary options like OSF , or data-specific repositories like DRYAD . This library guide provides more information on selecting an appropriate repository. The ASU Research Data Repository provides registered DOIs for published dataset records produced by ASU Researchers in the repository. ASU Library’s KEEP Institutional Repository provides persistent identifiers called Handles, which are similar to DOIs. Only scholarly works by ASU authors are accepted for both of these ASU repositories. For journals: If you are publishing a journal, the publisher will go through a DOI Registration Agency , such as CrossRef and DataCite in the U.S. For more detailed information about DOI registration and pricing, take a look at the DOI Foundation's Frequently Asked Questions about the DOI System .

Recall Due Dates and Notices When an item is recalled, the due date for that item automatically changes and a Recall Notice is sent. Recall of an item may shorten the original loan period. Recalls are due 3 weeks after the recall request, unless the original due date is within 3 weeks. Question: What is a peer-reviewed article?
Answer Text: Here is a link to our tutorial on this topic: Peer-Reviewed / Refereed Articles Peer reviewed articles are the major articles in academic journals or chapters in scholarly books that have undergone the "peer review" or refereed process before being accepted for publication. They are reviewed by other scholars in the same field or discipline for accuracy and completeness. Remember, only the MAJOR articles in a scholarly journal will be peer-reviewed! The following items in those same types of publications will NOT be peer-reviewed: letters to the editor editorials news

Question: My instructor wants us to use credible sources. How do I know what's good?
Answer Text: Many instructors want you to use factual information that appears in reputable scholarly journals or books rather than opinions or unreliable sources found online from an unknown party.  Most of our library databases let you limit your search to scholarly resources, but even those can include opinionated sections that may not be what your instructor wants. First, make sure you know what types of resources are acceptable for your assignment!  Sometimes newspaper articles are acceptable, but sometimes they are not! Book reviews in a scholarly journal may be ok, but maybe not! So check with your instructor or check your syllabus for specific resource types that are allowed or not allowed. Need to learn how to evaluate resources? Read the script or view the tutorial about Evaluating Resources!

Question: What do I do if I received the wrong item through Interlibrary Loan?
Answer Text: For articles: Reply to the original email notice telling us what the problem is and we’ll ask the lending library to send the correct article. For books or other loans: Please return it to Interlibrary Loan as soon as possible and tell us the problem. You can also call or email us, including the TN# and the problem, at (480) 965-3282 or ASU-ILL@asu.edu . The wrong item will be returned to the lending library and the correct one will be reordered. Interlibrary Loan cannot reorder material until it has been checked in and returned to the owning library.

Question: Why do I need to have good metadata and readme information?
Answer Text: To assist users in interpreting the data for potential replication or reuse you should add information that helps tell others exactly how you created and used your datasets. For example tabular data can still be hard to read if someone doesn't know what your column headers or variable names mean. Projects are varied and you should not assume someone can just figure out what you did. A readme file, code sheet, or data dictionary can help mitigate confusion and help your data align with our charge to make research data findable, accessible, discoverable, and reusable ( FAIR) . These elements can be uploaded directly into your dataset or added within the metadata. For more information see: How to make a Data Dictionary (Center for Open Science) Guide to writing "readme" style metadata ( Cornell University) ASU Library Data Management Tutorials

Question: How do I clear the cache and cookies in my web browser?
Answer Text: If you're experiencing issues accessing full-text library resources, clearing your browser's cache, cookies, and browsing history may resolve the problem. Follow these steps: Confirm the basics first: Are you a current ASU student, faculty, or staff member? Are you signed in to the ASU Library website ? If you answered "yes" to both, try the steps below to clear your cache. You may need to try both methods if the issue persists. Keyboard Shortcut for Most Browsers If you're using Internet Explorer, Edge, Google Chrome, or Mozilla Firefox, you can quickly clear your cache using a keyboard shortcut: Open your browser. Press Ctrl ( Cmd ) + Shift + Delete simultaneously on your keyboard. This will open a window where you can clear your cache, cookies, and browsing history. Select the appropriate options and proceed to clear the data. For time range, select " All time " or " Everything ." Close/quit the browser and restart it after clearing the cache and cookies. Additional Help by Browser: Clear cache in Chrome Clear cache in Firefox Clear cache in Safari Consider the following additional browser-related settings: Disable Browser Extensions : Some extensions, especially ad-blockers or privacy tools, can interfere with resource access. Temporarily disable them to see if access improves. Update Your Browse r: Ensure you're using the latest version of your browser, as outdated versions may have compatibility issues. Enable Cookies and JavaScript : Many library resources require cookies and JavaScript. Verify that these are enabled in your browser settings. Check Pop-Up Blocker Settings : Some resources open in new windows. Ensure your pop-up blocker is configured to allow pop-ups from library websites. Use a Supported Browser : While most modern browsers are compatible, some resources may work better with specific browsers. If you encounter issues, try accessing the resource using a different browser. Still having trouble? If these steps do not resolve the issue, please contact us for support. Be sure to include screenshots or a video that shows the full URL in the browser.

Question: I can't pull up the full text. What's up?
Answer Text: Many items are simply not available online in full text format. Within our journal subscriptions, some articles may not be available. Why not? An embargo period may be in effect (typically the most recent few months) for which we do not have access. Supplemental or other special issues of a journal may not be available to us as full text. This may be identifiable by a letter accompanying the page number within the citation. Specific articles may be restricted due to lack of copyright permissions from the author(s) of the article. For ASU Library resources available with full text online: Current ASU students, faculty and staff will need to be signed in with their ASURITE ID and password to access most of our full text options. Visitors to our campuses may access most of our online resources on-site using an ASU computer or using our "ASU Guest" WiFi. Click here to view our Remote Access policy . If the full text link takes you to the Journal's home page , try these options: Option 1: Drill down to the article by clicking through the article's year, volume, issue, starting page. Option 2: Look for a "search within this Journal" feature, where you can search for the article title. If you are still not connecting to full text: Current ASU students, faculty and staff may request a copy of the article, chapter, etc. through your Interlibrary Loan ILLIAD account. Our Interlibrary Loan library guide has a link to your ILLIAD account sign-in page. Articles and chapter scans generally take a few hours to a few days to arrive in your email! For help with a particular resource: Contact us through any of the methods listed our on Ask a Librarian page. To report broken links, error messages, and other technical issues: Contact us through the Get Help! icon on the Library Systems Support page.

If you are concerned about the quality of a journal, our Evaluating Publishing Options page provides a checklist of best practices every reputable publisher should follow. Question: What is the maximum file size I can upload to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer Text: Please review the following limitations for large file size upload to the ASU Research Data Repository : Single file upload: The maximum file size limit for individual file upload is 3GB. The experience for each user may vary based on the stability of the internet connection, speed of connection, file complexity, file format, and type; For tabular data files, additional processing by Dataverse means that more time is required to upload and this may result in upload failures depending on the file size, the number of columns, cases or rows, in your file. We recommend you upload files less than 500MB in size that requires this feature (see Dataverse Platform User Guide ); Multiple file upload: The repository accepts .zip or .tar file bundles. Automatically, the repository will unpack your single .zip package file and present individual files contained within to users without any hierarchy or organization maintained. Alternatively, .tar files or double-zipped files can be uploaded directly to Dataverse and will preserve your original file organization. Given the additional processing required for .zip files, we recommend you upload zip files that contain less than 500 files (see Advanced Dataverse Platform User Guide ); If you have many separate data files and/or metadata that you would like to upload to create or populate a Dataset or Dataverse, consider using the SWORD API (see Advanced Dataverse User Guide ), which can support batch upload of files (same size limits as described above). It is recommended that file size be factored in when considering data reuse. Larger files can be more difficult for researchers to handle and work with. Preparing and structuring your data for reuse requires careful consideration of potential use cases, the software required for analysis, the computer system, and file disk space. Dataverse is always developing to support more disciplines and data types, we anticipate that in the future larger datasets will be better supported and managed in Dataverse. If you have questions about file size limits, please contact Researcher Support .

Question: What happens to my ASU Research Data Repository account when I leave ASU?
Answer Text: Users departing the university still have ASURITE identities and single sign-on capability after they leave. However, they do not have access to all ASU provided resources that login gave them access to due to various policies and subscription contracts. We limit access to library subscription resources when you graduate or leave, but our repositories present a unique case where a person might need continued access after separating from the institution. We are still working through this question but if an owner of a collection (also known as a Dataverse) recognizes a user with an ASU ID they may be able to provide continued access for contributing to their respective collections and datasets, but this may not carry over to external contributors who’s temporary IDs have expired and not been renewed.

Question: Looking for Impact Information for articles/books
Answer Text: There are a few ways you can go about measuring the impact of your work. For article-level metrics, you can look for citation counts. Google Scholar is actually pretty good at this - just look for your article title in Google Scholar and the number of times your article has been cited will be listed in the entry. You can also look for citation counts in Scopus. ImpactStory is also a great service to get some non-traditional impact measurements for your work. Google Scholar is also one of the best ways to get citation counts for books- use the advanced search and languages limit in Settings for reducing the amount of unwanted results. Finally, the Metrics Toolkit can provide some great information about different sources for impact metrics, depending on type of work or measuring your metrics as an author.

Question: Are there due dates or fees for interlibrary loan items? Can I renew ILL items?
Answer Text: Yes, interlibrary loan items have due dates and overdue fees.  These differ depending on the item type and library who is lending the item to you. Due dates For physical items, you can check the due date on the strap attached to the item, or through your ILLiad account .  The average loan period for most physical items is between 4 and 8 weeks. Electronically delivered items like articles and ebooks do not need to be returned.  Your access will automatically expire after 60 days. Overdue fees Most ILL items are free to request, receive, and return. For interlibrary loan items that are not returned by the due date, there is a  there is a $5 per day late fee with a maximum overdue fee of $25, and a default $150.00 replacement fee. The replacement fee may be adjusted depending on the actual cost of the item billed by the library who owns the item.  We have a high overdue fee as many libraries will block us from requesting any materials when their items are returned late. Home Delivery items are shipped FedEx 2 Day, and include a FedEx Ground return label.  You are responsible for overdue, damaged, and lost items. Renewals Renewals are usually not available for interlibrary loan items. To have ILL staff negotiate an extension on a due date with the owning library, please contact us prior to the due date on the item. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill

Question: How do I access an ASU dissertation or thesis?
Answer Text: How do I access an ASU dissertation or thesis? Current ASU students, faculty and staff: The availability, location, and format of ASU dissertations and theses depends on the time period in which the student completed their degree. Check our ASU Dissertations and Theses library guide for more information on where to search. Other researchers: You can borrow copies of most of our dissertations and theses through your institutional library . We lend to academic libraries, public libraries and corporate libraries. More information about lending to other institutions can be found in our Interlibrary Loan library guide. You can also search the ProQuest Dissertation Express service to see if they have a copy of the item you need. Or you may contact University Archives to see if we have a copy. Our University Archives may be able to duplicate the entirety of a thesis or dissertation with a per page charge plus postage/handling. Email: archives@asu.edu For more information on obtaining ASU Dissertations and Theses, see our ASU Dissertations and Theses library guide.
